does: This paper introduces RoleRMBench, a systematic benchmark for evaluating reward models in role-playing dialogue systems, with seven fine-grained evaluation capabilities including narrative management, role consistency, and engagement. It proposes RoleRM, a novel reward model trained using Continuous Implicit Preferences (CIP), which reframes subjective evaluation as continuous consistent pairwise supervision under multiple structuring strategies. The authors demonstrate through comprehensive experiments that RoleRM substantially outperforms existing general-purpose reward models by over 24% on average, showing particular improvements in capturing narrative coherence and stylistic dimensions that matter in persona-grounded interactions.
connects: This work extends the line of reward modeling research that has become central to RLHF and LLM alignment, building on prior work in preference learning and human feedback incorporation. It responds directly to the recognized limitation that general-purpose reward models fail to capture subjective judgments in open-ended, persona-driven domains. The paper combines insights from dialogue systems research on role consistency and engagement with advances in preference learning methodology, particularly drawing on techniques for handling nuanced, continuous human preferences rather than binary comparisons, addressing a gap identified in applying existing alignment methods to creative and character-driven interaction scenarios.
enables: This work establishes infrastructure and methodology for developing better reward signals in creative language tasks beyond role play, including storytelling, character dialogue, and other subjective generation tasks. By demonstrating that specialized reward models with continuous preference formulation outperform general ones, it opens directions for domain-specific alignment approaches that preserve nuance in other open-ended applications. The benchmark and evaluation framework enable future research into persona-aware preference learning, potentially informing better methods for capturing human values in subjective tasks and supporting development of more realistic interactive dialogue systems that maintain character fidelity while satisfying user preferences.
